quote: Y'all prices are a little higher than these other companies for, like, list one other company I've looked at.
stewart_read: Patricia raises a direct price-comparison objection against Ion within the first minute. This is the call's highest-leverage moment and Ty does not respond to it — he pivots immediately to acknowledging the duplicate and offering to 'get that updated.' Strategic read: Ty may have been prioritizing duplicate resolution before addressing the objection. Reactive read: Ty simply missed the objection because the duplicate discovery consumed his attention. The reactive read holds — there is no evidence of a planned return to the price concern, and the call ends without it being addressed.
alternative_hypothesis: Ty was deliberately deferring the price objection until after resolving the duplicate confusion, intending to return to it once the situation was clarified.
coaching_implication: Show Ty the schema's already_saw_design rebuttal and the comparison_framing strategy. The price objection was live and uncontested. Ask him: 'What would you have said if the duplicate hadn't come up?' That answer tells you whether this is a knowledge gap or an attention gap.

quote: It looks like there was a duplicate here. I do see that you did end up speaking with with James yesterday. I'm not entirely sure too sure why there was a duplicate here.
stewart_read: Ty confirms the duplicate and expresses uncertainty about why it happened — in front of the customer. No duplicate-lead protocol exists in the schema; this is a floor-level process gap. The correct move here was to confirm whether Patricia's existing appointment with James was still on the calendar and offer to ensure continuity, not to apologize for the system and offer to 'get it updated.' Ty's handling makes Ion look disorganized at the exact moment Patricia is already comparing Ion unfavorably on price.
coaching_implication: Flag this for Kenny as a process gap: setters need a defined protocol for duplicate-lead calls. At minimum: (1) confirm the prior appointment status, (2) do not express confusion about internal systems in front of the customer, (3) use the duplicate as a continuity signal — 'I want to make sure you're taken care of' — not an apology.

quote: Like, third party vendors or whatever, it's like, what kind of house do you have? And that's the only question. I'm like, great. You're a good candidate. We've already sent all your information to all these companies.
stewart_read: Patricia is disclosing a genuine grievance — she went looking for information and was data-harvested by lead aggregators without her consent. This is a trust-gap disclosure that functions as a vulnerability signal: she feels manipulated by the solar industry's lead ecosystem. Ty's response ('No. I'm sorry about that.') acknowledges the frustration but provides no bridge. The schema's TBD data_mining_concern rebuttal is the closest analog — the gap is that no Ion-specific differentiating response exists yet. The correct move was to acknowledge specifically and then reframe: Patricia came to Ion directly (or was referred by Tesla), which is categorically different from a lead aggregator scraping her data.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to surface the data_mining_concern rebuttal gap for Kenny. In the 1-on-1 with Ty, ask: 'What's the difference between Ion calling Patricia and those aggregators selling her data?' If Ty can articulate it, he needs to say it on the call. If he can't, that's the coaching gap.

quote: I'm just I'm just trying to find information, like, what panels what solar panels are there, like, for how long does the average cost, like, all this stuff, and it's just instead. So
stewart_read: Patricia is not just venting — she is disclosing that she is actively researching solar and wants to understand panels, costs, and timelines. This is a genuine interest signal buried inside a frustration disclosure. A rep reading this correctly would have heard 'she wants information' and pivoted: 'That's exactly what our specialist walk-through covers — your specific panels, your specific numbers. That's the appointment James set up.' Instead, Ty exits the call. The enthusiasm signal goes completely unanchored.
coaching_implication: Show Ty this line in the 1-on-1. Patricia is telling him she wants what Ion sells — she just doesn't want to be data-farmed to get it. The pivot from her frustration to the existing appointment as the customer-controlled path to her actual goal was available and unused.

quote: I'll get that updated for you, and take this one off the list so we're no longer bothering these calls.
stewart_read: Ty closes the call by framing the outcome as removing Patricia from contact. This is not a scope-discipline failure in the technical sense — Ty is not doing closer-tier work — but it is a self-defeating close that abandons a lead Ion already invested in (James's prior visit). The correct close was to confirm the existing appointment with James, not to remove Patricia from the list. Ty is solving for his own discomfort with the duplicate situation rather than for the lead's value.
alternative_hypothesis: Ty may have determined that Patricia was not a viable lead given her price resistance and frustration, and was making a deliberate quality-over-quantity call. The reactive read holds — there is no evidence of a qualification-based DQ; Ty simply exits.
coaching_implication: Ask Ty: 'What happened to the appointment James set?' If it was still live, Ty just orphaned a sit. If it wasn't, Ty still had a warm lead expressing active interest in solar information and walked away from it. Either way, the close needed to be 'Let me make sure you're connected to James' not 'I'll take you off the list.'

quote: for, like, list one other company I've looked at.
stewart_read: Patricia mentions she has looked at at least one other company but Ty never asks which company or what design she saw. The verify.prior_designs item exists precisely to surface competitive context. Ty had the opening — Patricia volunteered the comparison — and did not take it. This is a minor gap given the call's brevity, but it compounds the price-objection miss: Ty has no competitive intelligence to work with even if he had chosen to address the price concern.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the price-objection coaching moment. Ty needed two pieces of information to handle the price concern: (1) which company, (2) what design/quote. He had neither because he didn't ask. The verify checklist exists to prevent exactly this gap.
